items.title: Labor’s ‘cruel’ algorithm-based aged care funding tool could have human override reinstated after Senate passes bill
items.standfirst: Tool used to determine level of home support funding has frequently under-assessed people and left them without adequate careFollow our Australia news live blog for latest updatesGet our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcastThe Senate has passed legislation to return human oversight to an aged care algorithm described by elderly Australians and their carers as “cruel” and “inhumane”, with the government conceding some decisions made by the tool need review.The integrated assessment tool (IAT), introduced in November, is used to determine the level of home support funding and care elderly people are entitled to. Continue reading...
items.content: Tool used to determine level of home support funding has frequently under-assessed people and left them without adequate careFollow our Australia news live blog for latest updatesGet our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcastThe Senate has passed legislation to return human oversight to an aged care algorithm described by elderly Australians and their carers as “cruel” and “inhumane”, with the government conceding some decisions made by the tool need review.The integrated assessment tool (IAT), introduced in November, is used to determine the level of home support funding and care elderly people are entitled to. items.title: The world is lurching from one economic crisis to the next. What should we expect from our government? | Nicki Hutley
items.standfirst: Tough times around the world mean governments are under pressure to provide more support. But just what is reasonable for us to ask?Follow our Australia news live blog for latest updatesGet our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcastThe pandemic changed a lot of things in Australia – and around the world – for better and worse. Two legacies that stand out to me from an economist’s perspective are an increased expectation on how governments should support us during troubled times, as well as an increased focus on domestic economic security, both with consequences for inflation and interest rates.The shortage of protective equipment, rapid tests and toilet paper during the early pandemic will not be forgotten easily and the recent jump in fuel prices, and especially images of some pumps running dry, brought back some of those painful memories.Nicki Hutley is a consulting economist Continue reading...
items.content: Tough times around the world mean governments are under pressure to provide more support. But just what is reasonable for us to ask?Follow our Australia news live blog for latest updatesGet our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcastThe pandemic changed a lot of things in Australia – and around the world – for better and worse. Two legacies that stand out to me from an economist’s perspective are an increased expectation on how governments should support us during troubled times, as well as an increased focus on domestic economic security, both with consequences for inflation and interest rates.The shortage of protective equipment, rapid tests and toilet paper during the early pandemic will not be forgotten easily and the recent jump in fuel prices, and especially images of some pumps running dry, brought back some of those painful memories.Nicki Hutley is a consulting economist Continue reading...

items.title: Lambie, Hanson and Pocock form unlikely alliance to protect transparency campaigner Rex Patrick
items.standfirst: Exclusive: Patrick is using freedom of information rules to seek where nuclear waste from Aukus submarines will be keptFollow our Australia news live blog for latest updatesGet our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcastJacqui Lambie, Pauline Hanson and David Pocock have joined a push for the government to stop a legal threat against Rex Patrick, after bureaucrats unexpectedly escalated a transparency case to the federal court.Patrick, a transparency campaigner and former senator, is seeking documents detailing where nuclear waste from the Aukus submarine fleet will be kept within Australia, and won an administrative appeal under freedom of information rules in May. Continue reading...
items.content: Exclusive: Patrick is using freedom of information rules to seek where nuclear waste from Aukus submarines will be keptFollow our Australia news live blog for latest updatesGet our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcastJacqui Lambie, Pauline Hanson and David Pocock have joined a push for the government to stop a legal threat against Rex Patrick, after bureaucrats unexpectedly escalated a transparency case to the federal court.Patrick, a transparency campaigner and former senator, is seeking documents detailing where nuclear waste from the Aukus submarine fleet will be kept within Australia, and won an administrative appeal under freedom of information rules in May. clusters.title: Venezuelans make desperate pleas for help a week after devastating double earthquake
clusters.neutral_headline: Venezuelans make desperate pleas for help a week after devastating double earthquake
bullet_summary: Venezuelans in the coastal city of La Guaira are pleading for heavy machinery to assist in ongoing rescue operations more than a week after a double earthquake devastated the area
5 quakes devastated the area one week ago and at least 2,200 people have died

items.title: Venezuelans make desperate pleas for help a week after devastating double earthquake
items.standfirst: Venezuelans in the coastal city of La Guaira are pleading for heavy machinery to assist in ongoing rescue operations more than a week after a double earthquake devastated the area. Magnitude 7.2 and 7.5 quakes devastated the area one week ago and at least 2,200 people have died. People on the ground have faced a desperate wait for help to find family members lost under the rubble. Frustration is growing in the community as they ask where the government is, and why help has not arrived.
items.content: Venezuelans in the coastal city of La Guaira are pleading for heavy machinery to assist in ongoing rescue operations more than a week after a double earthquake devastated the area. Magnitude 7.2 and 7.5 quakes devastated the area one week ago and at least 2,200 people have died. People on the ground have faced a desperate wait for help to find family members lost under the rubble. Frustration is growing in the community as they ask where the government is, and why help has not arrived.
